title: 10 best herbal teas for stomach discomfort

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra, also known as licorice, teas helps with stomach discomfort because of its anti-inflammatory properties. The tea's active compound, glycyrrhizin, soothes the stomach lining and reduces inflammation, making it an effective remedy for heartburn, acid reflux, and ulcers. remedy_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: A triterpenoid saponin, glycyrrhizin helps with stomach discomfort by inhibiting the activity of gastric acid and reducing inflammation in the stomach lining.

remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of Glycyrrhiza glabra are used to make teas for stomach discomfort due to their high content of glycyrrhizin, a compound that soothes and protects the digestive system.
Leaves: The leaves of Glycyrrhiza glabra are used to make teas for stomach discomfort due to their anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract.
Flowers: The flowers of Glycyrrhiza glabra are used to make teas for stomach discomfort due to their ability to calm and soothe the digestive system, reducing symptoms of indigestion and bloating.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 10-20 fresh roots of glycyrrhiza glabra for a 500ml batch of tea.
Clean and dry the roots at 50°c for 2 hours to remove excess moisture.
Slice the roots into thin pieces and weigh 20-30g for a standard serving.
Steep the sliced roots in 500ml of boiling water for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea and let it cool down to a comfortable temperature for consumption.

remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are commonly used in herbal medicine to treat digestive issues, including indigestion and flatulence, due to their antispasmodic properties.
Rhizomes: Rhizomes are rich in essential oils that help to reduce inflammation and spasms in the stomach, making them effective in soothing stomach discomfort.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh angelica archangelica leaves and stems or purchase dried angelica root and leaf mixture in the morning or evening.
Rinse 1 tablespoon of dried angelica root and leaf mixture or 2 tablespoons of fresh angelica leaves with cold water to remove impurities.
Boil 8 ounces of pure water in a kettle or pot for 5-7 minutes and then reduce heat to a simmer.
Steep the rinsed angelica mixture in the boiling water for 10-15 minutes, then strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a teapot or cup.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before drinking, sweetening with honey if desired, and enjoy within 30 minutes of preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon balm teas helps with stomach discomfort because of its natural calming properties that soothe digestive issues. The herb contains rosmarinic acid, which has anti-inflammatory effects that reduce inflammation in the gut and alleviate symptoms such as bloating, cramps, and diarrhea. Additionally, lemon balm's carminative properties help to dispel gas and ease indigestion. As a result, drinking lemon balm tea can provide quick relief from stomach discomfort, promoting a sense of calm and digestive balance.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic properties: Lemon balm tea helps relax the muscles in the digestive tract, reducing cramping and spasms associated with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), acid reflux, and other stomach disorders.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The tea's anti-inflammatory compounds reduce inflammation in the digestive system, which can help alleviate symptoms of gastrointestinal conditions such as Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis, and gastritis.

Gastrointestinal soothing properties: Lemon balm tea contains rosmarinic acid, an antioxidant that soothes and protects the mucous membranes lining the stomach and intestines, reducing discomfort, nausea, and heartburn.
remedy_parts: Roots: Lemon balm roots are used to make a calming tea that soothes digestive issues and alleviates stomach discomfort.
Leaves: The leaves of the lemon balm plant contain citral, an essential oil that has anti-inflammatory properties and helps to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried melissa officinalis leaves for every eight ounces of boiling water.
Heat filtered water in a teapot or kettle to a rolling boil for two to three minutes.
Add the measured melissa officinalis leaves to the boiling water and steep for five to seven minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a tea strainer or piece of cheesecloth to remove the herb residue.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before consuming within 30 minutes to one hour for optimal benefits.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Wild marjoram teas helps with stomach discomfort because of its natural anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. The tea's active compounds, such as bornyl acetate and thymol, have been shown to soothe digestive issues like bloating, cramps, and gas by reducing inflammation in the gut. Additionally, marjoram's carminative properties help to ease digestive spasms, promoting a sense of relaxation and comfort in the stomach area, allowing for smoother digestion and relief from discomfort.
remedy_properties: Carminative and anti-spasmodic properties: Wild marjoram tea helps to soothe digestive issues by reducing muscle spasms in the stomach and intestines, relieving cramps, bloating, and gas.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The flavonoids present in wild marjoram tea have potent anti-inflammatory effects, which can help to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) such as diarrhea, constipation, and abdominal pain.

Digestive enzyme stimulant properties: Wild marjoram tea has been shown to stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, such as amylase, lipase, and trypsin, which can help to improve nutrient absorption, reduce symptoms of indigestion, and alleviate bloating and discomfort.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are used due to their bitter and astringent properties, which help to soothe and calm digestive issues.
Seeds: The seeds contain compounds that have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic effects, making them effective in alleviating stomach cramps and discomfort.
remedy_recipe: Gather one teaspoon of dried oregano leaves and bring fresh water to a boil in a medium saucepan.
Add the dried oregano leaves to the boiling water and steep for 7-10 minutes to release its active compounds.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh strainer or cheesecloth to remove the solids.
Allow the tea to cool for 5-7 minutes before drinking it hot or warm to soothe digestive discomfort.
Consume one cup of oregano tea once daily as needed up to 3 times a week to promote stomach health.

supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to stomach discomfort that are treated with herbal teas are digestive issues such as constipation, diarrhea, and bloating. Herbal teas like peppermint, ginger, and chamomile can soothe irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) symptoms, while fennel and dill tea may alleviate gas and indigestion. Additionally, teas made from licorice root and slippery elm can provide relief for inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) and colitis sufferers. These natural remedies can be a gentle and effective way to promote digestive health and comfort.
intro_desc: Herbal teas for stomach discomfort are a natural remedy that helps alleviate symptoms of digestive issues such as bloating, nausea, and cramps. These teas are made from herbs like peppermint, chamomile, and ginger, which have anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the stomach and ease discomfort. Examples include peppermint tea, which can help alleviate IBS symptoms, and ginger tea, which can aid digestion and reduce nausea, improving quality of life and overall well-being for those who suffer from stomach issues.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal teas that help with stomach discomfort is a blend of peppermint, ginger, and chamomile. Peppermint tea soothes the digestive system, while ginger tea helps to reduce inflammation and alleviate nausea. Chamomile tea calms the stomach and promotes relaxation. This trio works in harmony to alleviate cramps, bloating, and digestive issues, making it an ideal combination for soothing stomach discomfort. Enjoying these teas as a daily brew can lead to a healthier and more balanced digestive system.
intro_study: For example, according to a study published by Complementary therapies in medicine, the use of herbal teas may be beneficial for stomach discomfort due to the high efficacy and safety profile of phytopreparations. The study, which analyzed data from 1137 patients, found that 10.4% of them used phytopreparations for gastrointestinal disorders, with the most common complaints being constipation (44%) and dyspepsia (23%). The results of the study suggest that herbal remedies, such as those containing laxatives, artichoke, and silymarin, may be effective in treating mild and moderate gastrointestinal disorders, making them a potential option for relieving stomach discomfort.
